use it to classify rational Hodge-Tate crystals by (log-) nearly Hodge-Tate
representations. Various cohomology comparison and vanishing results are proved
along the way.

Sensor technologies and data acquisition.title: Hodge-Tate prismatic crystals and Sen theory
Sensor technologies and data acquisition.summary:
Let $K$ be a mixed characteristic complete discrete valuation field with
perfect residue field, and let $K_\infty/K$ be a Kummer tower extension by
adjoining a compatible system of $p$-power roots of a chosen uniformizer. We
use this Kummer tower to reconstruct Sen theory which classically is obtained
using the cyclotomic tower. Using this Sen theory over the Kummer tower, we
prove a conjecture of Min-Wang which predicts that Hodge-Tate prismatic
crystals are determined by the Sen operator; this implies that the category of
(rational) Hodge-Tate prismatic crystals is equivalent to the category of
nearly Hodge-Tate representations.

System identification and validation in.title: Advancements in Crop Analysis through Deep Learning and Explainable AI
System identification and validation in.summary: Rice is a staple food of global importance in terms of trade, nutrition, and economic growth. Among Asian nations such as China, India, Pakistan, Thailand, Vietnam and Indonesia are leading producers of both long and short grain varieties, including basmati, jasmine, arborio, ipsala, and kainat saila. To ensure consumer satisfaction and strengthen national reputations, monitoring rice crops and grain quality is essential. Manual inspection, however, is labour intensive, time consuming and error prone, highlighting the need for automated solutions for quality control and yield improvement. This study proposes an automated approach to classify five rice grain varieties using Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN). A publicly available dataset of 75000 images was used for training and testing. Model evaluation employed accuracy, recall, precision, F1-score, ROC curves, and confusion matrices. Results demonstrated high classification accuracy with minimal misclassifications, confirming the model effectiveness in distinguishing rice varieties. In addition, an accurate diagnostic method for rice leaf diseases such as Brown Spot, Blast, Bacterial Blight, and Tungro was developed. The framework combined explainable artificial intelligence (XAI) with deep learning models including CNN, VGG16, ResNet50, and MobileNetV2. Explainability techniques such as SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) and LIME (Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations) revealed how specific grain and leaf features influenced predictions, enhancing model transparency and reliability. The findings demonstrate the strong potential of deep learning in agricultural applications, paving the way for robust, interpretable systems that can support automated crop quality inspection and disease diagnosis, ultimately benefiting farmers, consumers, and the agricultural economy.

System identification and validation in.title:
Machine Learning Approaches on Crop Pattern Recognition a Comparative
  Analysis
System identification and validation in.summary:
Monitoring agricultural activities is important to ensure food security.
Remote sensing plays a significant role for large-scale continuous monitoring
of cultivation activities. Time series remote sensing data were used for the
generation of the cropping pattern. Classification algorithms are used to
classify crop patterns and mapped agriculture land used. Some conventional
classification methods including support vector machine (SVM) and decision
trees were applied for crop pattern recognition. However, in this paper, we are
proposing Deep Neural Network (DNN) based classification to improve the
performance of crop pattern recognition and make a comparative analysis with
two (2) other machine learning approaches including Naive Bayes and Random
Forest.

System identification and validation in.title:
Advancing Wheat Crop Analysis: A Survey of Deep Learning Approaches
  Using Hyperspectral Imaging
System identification and validation in.summary:
As one of the most widely cultivated and consumed crops, wheat is essential
to global food security. However, wheat production is increasingly challenged
by pests, diseases, climate change, and water scarcity, threatening yields.
Traditional crop monitoring methods are labor-intensive and often ineffective
for early issue detection. Hyperspectral imaging (HSI) has emerged as a
non-destructive and efficient technology for remote crop health assessment.
However, the high dimensionality of HSI data and limited availability of
labeled samples present notable challenges. In recent years, deep learning has
shown great promise in addressing these challenges due to its ability to
extract and analysis complex structures. Despite advancements in applying deep
learning methods to HSI data for wheat crop analysis, no comprehensive survey
currently exists in this field. This review addresses this gap by summarizing
benchmark datasets, tracking advancements in deep learning methods, and
analyzing key applications such as variety classification, disease detection,
and yield estimation. It also highlights the strengths, limitations, and future
opportunities in leveraging deep learning methods for HSI-based wheat crop
analysis. We have listed the current state-of-the-art papers and will continue
tracking updating them in the following
https://github.com/fadi-07/Awesome-Wheat-HSI-DeepLearning.

System identification and validation in.title: Spatio-temporal Crop Classification On Volumetric Data
System identification and validation in.summary:
Large-area crop classification using multi-spectral imagery is a widely
studied problem for several decades and is generally addressed using classical
Random Forest classifier. Recently, deep convolutional neural networks (DCNN)
have been proposed. However, these methods only achieved results comparable
with Random Forest. In this work, we present a novel CNN based architecture for
large-area crop classification. Our methodology combines both spatio-temporal
analysis via 3D CNN as well as temporal analysis via 1D CNN. We evaluated the
efficacy of our approach on Yolo and Imperial county benchmark datasets. Our
combined strategy outperforms both classical as well as recent DCNN based
methods in terms of classification accuracy by 2% while maintaining a minimum
number of parameters and the lowest inference time.

System identification and validation in.title: Exploring Self-Attention for Crop-type Classification Explainability
authors:
Dario Augusto Borges Oliveira and Xiao
  Xiang Zhu
System identification and validation in.summary:
Transformer models have become a promising approach for crop-type
classification. Although their attention weights can be used to understand the
relevant time points for crop disambiguation, the validity of these insights
depends on how closely the attention weights approximate the actual workings of
these black-box models, which is not always clear. In this paper, we introduce
a novel explainability framework that systematically evaluates the explanatory
power of the attention weights of a standard transformer encoder for crop-type
classification. Our results show that attention patterns strongly relate to key
dates, which are often associated with critical phenological events for
crop-type classification. Further, the sensitivity analysis reveals the limited
capability of the attention weights to characterize crop phenology as the
identified phenological events depend on the other crops considered during
training. This limitation highlights the relevance of future work towards the
development of deep learning approaches capable of automatically learning the
temporal vegetation dynamics for accurate crop disambiguation

3D reconstruction methods for crop grain.title:
AGSPNet: A framework for parcel-scale crop fine-grained semantic change
  detection from UAV high-resolution imagery with agricultural geographic scene
  constraints
3D reconstruction methods for crop grain.summary:
Real-time and accurate information on fine-grained changes in crop
cultivation is of great significance for crop growth monitoring, yield
prediction and agricultural structure adjustment. Aiming at the problems of
serious spectral confusion in visible high-resolution unmanned aerial vehicle
(UAV) images of different phases, interference of large complex background and
salt-and-pepper noise by existing semantic change detection (SCD) algorithms,
in order to effectively extract deep image features of crops and meet the
demand of agricultural practical engineering applications, this paper designs
and proposes an agricultural geographic scene and parcel-scale constrained SCD
framework for crops (AGSPNet). AGSPNet framework contains three parts:
agricultural geographic scene (AGS) division module, parcel edge extraction
module and crop SCD module. Meanwhile, we produce and introduce an UAV image
SCD dataset (CSCD) dedicated to agricultural monitoring, encompassing multiple
semantic variation types of crops in complex geographical scene. We conduct
comparative experiments and accuracy evaluations in two test areas of this
dataset, and the results show that the crop SCD results of AGSPNet consistently
outperform other deep learning SCD models in terms of quantity and quality,
with the evaluation metrics F1-score, kappa, OA, and mIoU obtaining
improvements of 0.038, 0.021, 0.011 and 0.062, respectively, on average over
the sub-optimal method. The method proposed in this paper can clearly detect
the fine-grained change information of crop types in complex scenes, which can
provide scientific and technical support for smart agriculture monitoring and
management, food policy formulation and food security assurance.

Sensor technologies and data acquisition.title:
Data Acquisition Architecture and Online Processing System for the HAWC
  gamma-ray observatory
Sensor technologies and data acquisition.summary:
The High Altitude Water Cherenkov observatory (HAWC) is an air shower array

Sensor technologies and data acquisition.title:
Integrated Data Acquisition, Storage, Retrieval and Processing Using the
  COMPASS DataBase (CDB)
Sensor technologies and data acquisition.summary:
We present a complex data handling system for the COMPASS tokamak, operated
by IPP ASCR Prague, Czech Republic [1]. The system, called CDB (Compass
DataBase), integrates different data sources as an assortment of data
acquisition hardware and software from different vendors is used. Based on
widely available open source technologies wherever possible, CDB is vendor and
platform independent and it can be easily scaled and distributed. The data is
directly stored and retrieved using a standard NAS (Network Attached Storage),
hence independent of the particular technology; the description of the data
(the metadata) is recorded in a relational database. Database structure is
general and enables the inclusion of multi-dimensional data signals in multiple
revisions (no data is overwritten). This design is inherently distributed as
the work is off-loaded to the clients. Both NAS and database can be implemented
and optimized for fast local access as well as secure remote access. CDB is
implemented in Python language; bindings for Java, C/C++, IDL and Matlab are
provided. Independent data acquisitions systems as well as nodes managed by
FireSignal [2] are all integrated using CDB. An automated data post-processing
server is a part of CDB. Based on dependency rules, the server executes, in
parallel if possible, prescribed post-processing tasks.

Sensor technologies and data acquisition.title: Proposal for fiber optic data acquisition system for Baikal-GVD
authors:
D. N.
  Zaborov (for the Baikal-GVD Collaboration)
Sensor technologies and data acquisition.summary:
The first stage of the construction of the deep underwater neutrino telescope
Baikal-GVD is planned to be completed in 2024. The second stage of the detector
deployment is planned to be carried out using a data acquisition system based
on fibre optic technologies, which will allow for increased data throughput and
more flexible trigger conditions. A dedicated test facility has been built and
deployed at the Baikal-GVD site to test the new technological solutions. We
present the principles of operation and results of tests of the new data
acquisition system.

Sensor technologies and data acquisition.title: The Control Unit of the KM3NeT Data Acquisition System
Sensor technologies and data acquisition.summary:
The KM3NeT Collaboration runs a multi-site neutrino observatory in the
Mediterranean Sea. Water Cherenkov particle detectors, deep in the sea and far
off the coasts of France and Italy, are already taking data while incremental
construction progresses. Data Acquisition Control software is operating
off-shore detectors as well as testing and qualification stations for their
components. The software, named Control Unit, is highly modular. It can undergo
upgrades and reconfiguration with the acquisition running. Interplay with the
central database of the Collaboration is obtained in a way that allows for data
taking even if Internet links fail. In order to simplify the management of
computing resources in the long term, and to cope with possible hardware
failures of one or more computers, the KM3NeT Control Unit software features a
custom dynamic resource provisioning and failover technology, which is
especially important for ensuring continuity in case of rare transient events
in multi-messenger astronomy. The software architecture relies on ubiquitous
tools and broadly adopted technologies and has been successfully tested on
several operating systems.

paper_summaries.sub_topic: System identification and validation in agricultural engineering for crop analysis
paper_summaries.summary: Recent advancements in system identification for crop analysis leverage deep learning and explainable AI to enhance accuracy and interpretability. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have demonstrated high performance in classifying rice grain varieties and diagnosing leaf diseases, with explainability techniques like SHAP and LIME providing transparency into model decisions [ref2]. For crop pattern recognition, Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) outperform traditional classifiers such as Naive Bayes and Random Forest, especially when applied to time series remote sensing data [ref3]. In wheat crop analysis, hyperspectral imaging (HSI) combined with deep learning enables non-destructive, efficient health assessment, though challenges remain due to high data dimensionality and limited labeled samples [ref5]. Spatio-temporal crop classification benefits from hybrid CNN architectures that integrate 3D and 1D convolutions, achieving superior accuracy and efficiency on benchmark datasets [ref7]. Transformer-based models introduce self-attention mechanisms for crop-type classification, but their explainability is limited by dependencies on training data composition, underscoring the need for more robust temporal dynamic learning [ref16].
gaps: Limited generalizability and interpretability of deep learning models across diverse crop types and regions
Challenges in handling high-dimensional hyperspectral data with scarce labeled samples
Insufficient integration of temporal vegetation dynamics in current explainability frameworks
Lack of standardized benchmarks for cross-comparison of system identification methods in crop analysis

paper_summaries.sub_topic: 3D reconstruction methods for crop grains in agricultural engineering
paper_summaries.summary: Recent advancements in agricultural engineering have leveraged high-resolution UAV imagery and deep learning frameworks to enhance the detection and monitoring of crop changes at a fine-grained level. The AGSPNet framework introduces a novel approach by integrating agricultural geographic scene constraints and parcel-scale segmentation to address challenges such as spectral confusion, complex backgrounds, and noise in UAV images. This method comprises three core modules: agricultural geographic scene division, parcel edge extraction, and a crop semantic change detection (SCD) module. The introduction of the CSCD dataset, specifically tailored for agricultural monitoring, enables robust evaluation of semantic variation types in diverse geographical contexts. Experimental results demonstrate that AGSPNet significantly outperforms existing deep learning SCD models, achieving notable improvements in F1-score, kappa, overall accuracy, and mean intersection over union. These advancements provide a foundation for more accurate crop monitoring, yield prediction, and informed agricultural management, supporting the broader goals of smart agriculture and food security [ref1].
gaps: Integration of 3D reconstruction techniques with semantic change detection for crop grains remains unexplored.
Limited evaluation on diverse crop types and varying environmental conditions.
Real-time deployment and scalability of the proposed framework in large-scale agricultural settings.
Fusion of multi-modal data (e.g., LiDAR, multispectral) for enhanced 3D crop grain modeling.

paper_summaries.sub_topic: Sensor technologies and data acquisition for 3D crop grain phenotyping
paper_summaries.summary: Recent advances in sensor technologies and data acquisition architectures, though primarily developed for high-throughput physics experiments, offer valuable insights for 3D crop grain phenotyping. The HAWC gamma-ray observatory demonstrates a scalable, dead-time-free data acquisition system using photomultiplier tubes, TDCs, and high-throughput real-time processing, achieving minimal latency and robust parallel data analysis [ref14]. Similarly, the COMPASS DataBase (CDB) integrates heterogeneous data sources and acquisition hardware, leveraging open-source technologies for scalable, distributed, and platform-independent data storage and retrieval, with automated post-processing capabilities [ref5]. The Baikal-GVD project proposes a fiber optic-based data acquisition system, enabling increased throughput and flexible triggering, validated through dedicated test facilities [ref3]. The KM3NeT observatory’s modular Control Unit software ensures resilient, dynamic resource provisioning and failover, supporting continuous data acquisition even under network disruptions [ref1]. Collectively, these systems highlight the importance of modularity, scalability, and real-time processing in designing robust sensor and data acquisition platforms for complex, high-volume phenotyping applications.
gaps: Adaptation of high-throughput physics DAQ architectures to agricultural phenotyping sensor networks
Integration of multi-modal sensor data (e.g., imaging, spectroscopy) for 3D crop grain analysis
Real-time, in-field data processing and feedback for phenotyping applications
Scalable, open-source frameworks tailored to agricultural data acquisition and storage needs

Foundations of 3D Reconstruction.body: This scientific article presents a comprehensive method for phenotypic analysis of wheat throughout its entire growth cycle, from seedling to grain filling stages, using multi-view image sequences and 3D reconstruction. The researchers developed a workflow based on Structure-from-Motion and Multi-View Stereo (SFM-MVS) to generate dense point clouds of wheat plants. Key steps include constructing a multi-view image acquisition platform, preprocessing point clouds with improved Euclidean clustering and color/statistical filtering, and segmenting plant organs using a region growing algorithm. The method was tested on three wheat cultivars (FuMai1, PinHong1, PinBai1) grown in China, with phenotypic parameters such as plant height, leaf length, and leaf width extracted and validated against manual measurements. The results showed high accuracy (coefficients of determination >0.995, low RMSE and RRMSE), enabling rapid, non-destructive, and precise phenotypic monitoring. The study also analyzed growth dynamics and differences among cultivars using ANOVA, demonstrating the method's utility for breeding and crop monitoring. Limitations include reduced segmentation performance during stages with dense tillering and overlapping leaves, but future improvements are proposed, such as integrating neural network models for fully automated segmentation. The approach offers a cost-effective, high-throughput solution for wheat phenotyping and has implications for precision agriculture and breeding programs.

Foundations of 3D Reconstruction.body: This scientific article presents an integrated method for full-cycle phenotypic analysis of wheat using multi-view image sequences and 3D reconstruction. The study addresses the challenges of monitoring wheat growth due to complex plant structures, such as extensive tillering and overlapping organs. Researchers developed a workflow based on Structure-from-Motion and Multi-View Stereo (SFM-MVS) to generate dense 3D point clouds of wheat from seedling to grain filling stages. Key steps include multi-view image acquisition, point cloud reconstruction and scaling, noise removal using improved Euclidean clustering with centroid calculation, and stem-leaf segmentation via a region growing algorithm. The method was tested on three winter wheat cultivars (FuMai1, PinHong1, PinBai1) grown in China, with phenotypic parameters (plant height, leaf length, leaf width, crown area, surface area, convex volume) extracted and validated against manual measurements. Results showed high accuracy (R² > 0.995 for key traits), with root mean square errors for plant height, leaf length, and leaf width of 1.08 cm, 0.26 cm, and 0.03 cm, respectively. The workflow enabled dynamic monitoring of growth and significant differences among cultivars using ANOVA. While segmentation was less effective during stages with dense tillering and overlapping leaves, the method overall provides a rapid, non-destructive, and precise approach for wheat phenotyping, with potential applications in breeding and precision agriculture. Future improvements may involve deep learning for fully automated segmentation.

Foundations of 3D Reconstruction.body: This article presents the challenges and applications of structure-from-motion (SfM) photogrammetry in a laboratory flume model of a braided gravel-bed river. The authors describe methods for collecting over 1,000 high-quality digital elevation models (DEMs) with ~1 mm vertical precision using close-range SfM and Agisoft PhotoScan software. Key methodological advances include automatic target detection, batch processing, and strict attention to image quality (notably camera focus and lighting). The flume experiments, conducted at the University of Western Ontario, involved repeated imaging of an 18.3 m x 3 m model under varying stream power conditions, with cameras mounted on a movable trolley. Precision was validated through duplicate surveys, analysis of non-moving flat areas, and comparison with high-resolution laser scans. Challenges addressed include the doming effect in DEMs, target detection, camera settings, and processing time. Applications of the resulting DEMs include quantifying morphological change (using DEMs of difference), estimating water depth, and mapping bed surface texture and grain size using image texture analysis. The study demonstrates that SfM photogrammetry can efficiently produce large volumes of high-quality topographic data for geomorphological analysis in laboratory settings.

Mathematical Modeling of Crop Grains.body: This scientific article presents a novel, high-throughput method for extracting 3D shape traits and identifying filled versus unfilled cereal grains (rice, wheat, corn) using structured light imaging and point cloud analysis. The system uses a structured light scanner mounted on a robotic arm to capture high-precision 3D point clouds of grains placed vertically for optimal accuracy. The pipeline includes preprocessing, segmentation, and extraction of 25 phenotypic traits (length, width, thickness, volume, surface area, etc.), followed by machine learning-based classification. Experimental validation on 2200 grains showed average measurement errors of 2.07% (length), 0.97% (width), and 1.13% (thickness), with an average processing time of 9.6 seconds per grain—significantly faster than manual methods. The best model (XGBoost) achieved 90.18% accuracy in distinguishing filled from unfilled grains, and SVM achieved 99.95% accuracy in classifying rice subspecies (indica vs. japonica), though variety-level classification was less accurate (47.25%). Thickness was found to be the most important trait for filled/unfilled classification. The method is efficient, accurate, and non-destructive, offering significant benefits for crop breeding and genetic research.

Mathematical Modeling of Crop Grains.body: This scientific article compares the phenotypic variation of 102 Triticum durum (durum wheat) accessions from gene banks with 12 contemporary spring cultivars and Kamut® wheat, focusing on kernel shape and color using digital image analysis. The study, conducted in Poland, aimed to assess variation in grain traits and technological quality relevant to breeding. Key findings include: gene bank accessions had higher average protein (16.48% vs. 14.56%), wet gluten (38.04% vs. 32.07%), Zeleny sedimentation values (69.7 vs. 60.4 ml), and flour strength (W index 404.64 vs. 353.47) than cultivars, but lower yields (22.6 dt/ha vs. 46.8 dt/ha) and less desirable grain color (lower carotenoid concentration). Shape descriptors showed accessions had smaller, more elongated kernels, but crease depth was similar to cultivars. Color descriptors, especially yellowness (b*), were more strongly correlated with processing quality than shape. The study concludes that while gene bank accessions offer valuable genetic diversity for breeding, their low yield and poor color limit direct use; however, these traits can be improved through breeding. Digital image analysis is highlighted as an effective tool for rapid phenotypic assessment in wheat breeding.

Mathematical Modeling of Crop Grains.body: This scientific article examines the phenotypic variation among Triticum durum (durum wheat) genotypes stored in gene banks, using digital image analysis to assess kernel shape and color descriptors. The study compared 102 accessions and 12 contemporary cultivars of T. durum, as well as Kamut® wheat (T. turanicum), focusing on grain traits and technological quality indicators. Results showed that gene bank accessions had higher average protein (16.48% vs. 14.56%), wet gluten (38.04% vs. 32.07%), Zeleny sedimentation values (69.7 vs. 60.4 ml), and flour strength (W index: 404.64 vs. 353.47) compared to reference cultivars. However, accessions had lower yields (22.6 dt/ha) and less desirable grain color, indicating lower carotenoid content. Kernel size descriptors, such as image area and minimal Feret diameter, varied significantly, while crease depth did not. The study found that processing suitability traits correlated more strongly with color than shape, suggesting color parameters are useful for selecting high-quality breeding material. Despite their weaknesses, gene bank accessions offer valuable genetic diversity for breeding, and traits like yield and carotenoid content could be improved through targeted breeding strategies.

Mathematical Modeling of Crop Grains.body: This review article discusses the advancements in high-throughput plant phenotyping and phenotype data analysis, which are crucial for improving crop yields and resilience in the face of global food security challenges and climate change. The authors highlight the need for novel approaches to quantitatively analyze plant traits and link them to genetic information, enabling the breeding of high-yield, stress-tolerant crops. The article details the development and application of advanced phenotyping platforms that use non-destructive imaging technologies—such as visible light, infrared, fluorescence, hyperspectral imaging, CT, PET, and MRI—to capture complex plant traits at various scales. It also reviews the experimental setups, data collection workflows, and a range of open-source software tools for image processing and trait analysis. Key statistical and computational methods for phenotype data analysis are described, including dimensionality reduction (PCA, FA), model selection, relationship measurement, network analysis, growth modeling, classification, and clustering. The review concludes by emphasizing the need for more efficient, cost-effective, and adaptable data analysis infrastructures to handle the large-scale, high-dimensional datasets generated by modern phenomics, and calls for improved statistical methods and digital platforms to advance plant science and crop improvement.

Sensor Technologies and Data Acquisition.body: This scientific article presents a novel, intelligent method for analyzing 3D wheat grain and ventral sulcus traits using structured light imaging. Traditional manual measurement of wheat grain traits is inefficient and often destructive, especially for ventral sulcus features. The proposed method uses a structured light scanner to obtain 3D point cloud data of wheat grains, followed by specialized algorithms for single grain segmentation and ventral sulcus localization. The system extracts 28 3D phenotypic traits and 4 ventral sulcus traits from wheat grains. Orthogonal experiments determined optimal scanning conditions (30° rotation, 37° scanning angle, black stage color), and the method achieved high accuracy, with mean absolute percentage errors (MAPEs) of 1.83% for length, 1.86% for width, 2.19% for thickness, and 4.81% for ventral sulcus depth. The method was validated against X-ray CT imaging and manual measurements, showing comparable accuracy but with higher efficiency and non-destructive analysis. Using data from 500 grains across five wheat varieties, machine learning models (notably gradient boosting regression) predicted grain weight with R² up to 0.83 and MAPE as low as 3.37%. The entire workflow is integrated into specialized software, freely available for non-commercial research, enabling automated, high-throughput, and precise wheat grain phenotyping and weight prediction, which is valuable for wheat breeding and yield improvement.

Sensor Technologies and Data Acquisition.body: This scientific article presents a novel, non-destructive method for analyzing 3D wheat grain and ventral sulcus traits using structured light imaging. Traditional manual methods for measuring wheat grain traits are inefficient and often destructive, especially for ventral sulcus analysis. The proposed method uses a structured light scanner to obtain 3D point cloud data of wheat grains, followed by specialized algorithms for single grain segmentation and ventral sulcus localization. The system extracts 28 3D phenotypic characters and 4 ventral sulcus traits from wheat grains. Orthogonal experiments determined optimal scanning conditions (30° rotation, 37° scanning angle, black stage color), and the method was validated on 125 grains from 5 wheat varieties. The mean absolute percentage errors (MAPEs) for key measurements (length, width, thickness, ventral sulcus depth) ranged from 1.83% to 4.81%. The method's accuracy was comparable to X-ray CT imaging but with higher efficiency and lower cost. Using 500 grains, machine learning models (notably gradient boosting regression) predicted grain weight with R² up to 0.83. The entire pipeline was integrated into user-friendly software, enabling automated extraction and visualization of wheat grain traits and weight prediction, significantly improving efficiency for wheat breeding research.

Sensor Technologies and Data Acquisition.body: This scientific article presents a photogrammetry-based framework (using Structure from Motion, SfM) for measuring the volume and estimating the particle density of Lightweight Expanded Clay Aggregate (LECA), a porous material widely used in construction and geotechnics. The study compares the accuracy of this method with computed tomography (CT) and the traditional Archimedes’ water displacement method. Six LECA grains of different shapes were analyzed. The photogrammetry workflow involved photographing each grain from multiple angles, reconstructing 3D models, and post-processing to compute the envelope volume. The CT method provided a reference by capturing both external and internal structures, while the Archimedes’ method was found to overestimate density due to water infiltration into pores. Results showed that the photogrammetry-based method closely matched CT measurements, with a mean error of -0.33% (0.0016 g/cm³), while the Archimedes’ method overestimated density by 2.49% (0.0120 g/cm³). For 95% of the grain surface, the photogrammetry method achieved a reconstruction error of less than 0.129 mm for irregular grains and 0.073 mm for round grains. The study concludes that the photogrammetry-based approach is accurate, cost-effective, and suitable for measuring the particle density of porous aggregates, providing valuable data for laboratory testing and calibration of numerical models such as the Discrete Element Method (DEM).

Sensor Technologies and Data Acquisition.body:
This scientific article presents a comprehensive dataset of 2D and 3D scans of granular rock material heaps and calibration models, published in Scientific Data on March 27, 2025. The study focuses on the accurate measurement of the angle of repose (AoR), a key property in understanding the stability and movement of granular materials in fields such as mining, geotechnical engineering, pharmaceuticals, and agriculture. The dataset includes photographs and processed models of 86 heaps made from materials like dolomite, quartz sand, marble, and gravel, as well as one ideal physical cone and four CAD-designed models for calibration. 

The heaps were formed using a lifting cylinder method with a specially designed device to ensure repeatability and minimize human error. Both 2D image analysis (using ImageJ) and 3D photogrammetry (using Agisoft PhotoScan) were employed to create detailed models, with STL and point-cloud files available for each sample. The study describes several testing variations to assess the influence of different photographic and measurement setups. Validation was performed using a 3D-printed calibration cone and error checking of marker coordinates, confirming the accuracy and repeatability of the models. The dataset, available on Figshare, enables standardized comparison and calibration of AoR measurement methods, supporting the development of more reliable and consistent analysis techniques for granular materials.

Sensor Technologies and Data Acquisition.body: This scientific article presents a new, non-destructive strategy for detecting maize seed vigor using seed phenotyping and machine learning, with the goal of accelerating maize breeding. The study used 368 genetically diverse maize inbred lines and evaluated seed vigor through the cold-soaking germination test, which closely correlates with field emergence. Three non-destructive phenotyping methods were compared: hyperspectral imaging, machine vision, and gas chromatography-ion mobility spectrometry (GC-IMS). Machine vision, combined with machine learning algorithms, was identified as the optimal method, achieving about 90% accuracy in distinguishing high- and low-vigor seeds. Four key phenotypic features (B_mean, b_mean, S_mean, and b_std) were identified as most predictive of seed vigor. Genome-wide association studies (GWAS) linked these features to candidate genes involved in hormone regulation affecting seed germination. Metabolite analyses revealed significant differences in endogenous hormones, flavonoids, chlorophyll, and anthocyanidins between high- and low-vigor seeds. The study concludes that this non-destructive, high-throughput approach is reliable and can facilitate gene mining and the breeding of high-vigor maize varieties.

System Identification and Validation.body: This scientific article presents the development and validation of a high-throughput, non-destructive 3D reconstruction system for measuring plant part features in seedling phenotyping. The system uses a shape-from-silhouette method with 10 cameras to rapidly generate 3D models of seedlings, enabling accurate segmentation and measurement of plant organs such as stems and leaves. Key features measured include stem length, leaf length, leaf width, and leaf area. The method was tested on 541 tomato and bell pepper seedlings, with results compared to ground truth measurements obtained by manual 2D scanning after destructive sampling. The 3D system achieved high correlations with ground truth for stem length (correlation coefficient 0.87, RMSE 4.3 mm, average relative error 21%), leaf length (correlations 0.91 and 0.90 for first two leaves, RMSE 4.3 and 3.6 mm, relative errors 18% and 16%), leaf width (correlations 0.83 and 0.85, RMSE 2.1 and 1.9 mm, relative errors 30% and 27%), and leaf area (correlations 0.85 and 0.88, RMSE 27 and 24 mm², relative errors 22% and 19%). The system processes 3D reconstructions in 20–60 ms, making it suitable for large-scale phenotyping. Limitations include underestimation of measurements due to geometric approximations and occasional segmentation errors, especially for more complex leaf structures. The authors suggest future improvements in segmentation and measurement algorithms to further enhance accuracy and throughput.

System Identification and Validation.body: This scientific article presents a novel automated approach for diagnosing citrus leaf diseases, particularly in lemons, using advanced image enhancement and a nonlinear fuzzy rank-based ensemble (NL-FuRBE) method. The study addresses the limitations of traditional manual and automated disease detection methods, which are often slow, subjective, and ineffective at early stages. The proposed methodology enhances image quality using Vector-Valued Anisotropic Diffusion (VAD) and morphological filtering, evaluated by PSNR, SSIM, and NIQE metrics. Three deep learning models—VGG19, AlexNet, and Xception—are combined using a fuzzy rank-based scoring system with nonlinear transformations to improve classification robustness and handle prediction uncertainty. The approach was tested on a new lemon leaf disease dataset (1,354 images, 9 classes) and achieved an average accuracy of 96.51% with five-fold cross-validation, outperforming conventional and state-of-the-art methods. The ensemble model also demonstrated faster inference times and improved reliability across all disease categories. The study concludes that integrating image enhancement with a fuzzy ensemble significantly boosts the accuracy and efficiency of automated citrus disease detection, offering a cost-effective tool for precision agriculture. The dataset used is publicly available, and the method is positioned as a new benchmark for real-time, scalable disease diagnosis in citrus farming.

Applications and Case Studies.body: This scientific article presents a comprehensive method for phenotypic analysis of wheat throughout its entire growth cycle, from seedling to grain filling stages, using multi-view image sequences and 3D reconstruction. The researchers developed a workflow based on Structure-from-Motion and Multi-View Stereo (SFM-MVS) to generate dense point clouds of wheat plants, enabling non-destructive, rapid, and accurate extraction of key phenotypic parameters such as plant height, leaf length, and leaf width. The method involves constructing a multi-view image acquisition platform, preprocessing point clouds with improved Euclidean clustering and color/statistical filtering, and segmenting plant organs using a region growing algorithm. The approach was validated on three wheat cultivars, showing high accuracy (coefficients of determination >0.995, low RMSE and RRMSE values) when compared to manual measurements. The study also analyzed growth dynamics and differences among cultivars using ANOVA, demonstrating the method's effectiveness for dynamic monitoring and variety differentiation. Limitations include challenges in segmenting overlapping leaves during certain growth stages, with future improvements suggested via neural network-based segmentation. The method offers significant potential for high-throughput, precise phenotyping in wheat breeding and precision agriculture.

Applications and Case Studies.body: This scientific article presents a method for automated extraction of pod phenotype data from 3D micro-computed tomography (µCT) images of oilseed rape (Brassica napus) pods using a fine-tuned StarDist-3D deep learning model. The study addresses the bottleneck of manual phenotyping by applying machine vision to accurately detect, segment, and analyze seeds within pods. The StarDist-3D model, trained on annotated µCT images, achieved high accuracy in seed detection (Validation F1-score = 96.3%, Testing F1-score = 99.3%) and shape prediction (mean matched score = 90%). The pipeline enables rapid, non-destructive extraction of seed number, size, shape, spatial arrangement, and location within pod valves, outperforming manual methods in speed and data richness. The approach also includes an automated method for sorting seeds into pod valves using regression analysis. The method is generalizable to other plant species and structures that can be described as star-convex polygons. The study concludes that this automated pipeline can significantly enhance high-throughput plant phenotyping and crop yield modeling, with potential applications in plant breeding and developmental biology.

Applications and Case Studies.title: Grain structure control during metal 3D printing by high-intensity ...
Applications and Case Studies.snippet: Jan 9, 2020 ... Using the titanium alloy Ti-6Al-4V as a model alloy, we employ high-intensity ultrasound to achieve full transition from columnar grains to fine ...
Applications and Case Studies.body: This Nature Communications article (published January 9, 2020) presents a breakthrough in controlling grain structure during metal additive manufacturing (AM), specifically metal 3D printing, using high-intensity ultrasound. Traditionally, metal AM processes like laser-based directed energy deposition (DED) produce columnar grain structures, leading to anisotropic and often inferior mechanical properties. The researchers demonstrate that applying high-intensity ultrasound during the AM process of titanium alloy Ti-6Al-4V transforms the grain structure from coarse columnar to fine (~100 µm) equiaxed grains, resulting in a 12% improvement in both yield stress and tensile strength, without altering the alloy's chemistry. The method was also successfully applied to the nickel-based superalloy Inconel 625, indicating its general applicability. The ultrasound induces acoustic cavitation in the melt pool, enhancing nucleation and grain refinement. The study details the experimental setup, microstructural analysis, mechanical testing, and discusses practical considerations for scaling the technique, such as the choice of ultrasound transducer. The findings suggest this approach could be extended to other alloys and AM processes, offering a new pathway to improve the mechanical performance and reliability of 3D-printed metal components.

web_summaries.summary: 3D reconstruction forms the backbone of modern phenotypic analysis and environmental modeling, leveraging techniques such as Structure-from-Motion (SfM) and Multi-View Stereo (MVS) to generate dense point clouds from multi-view image sequences. In plant science, integrated workflows have been developed for full-cycle phenotypic analysis of wheat, enabling non-destructive, high-throughput extraction of traits like plant height, leaf length, and width with high accuracy (R² > 0.995) [An integrated method for phenotypic analysis of wheat based on ..., An integrated method for phenotypic analysis of wheat based ... - PMC]. These methods involve careful image acquisition, advanced point cloud preprocessing (e.g., improved Euclidean clustering, color/statistical filtering), and organ segmentation (region growing algorithms). Challenges such as dense tillering and overlapping organs remain, but future directions include neural network-based segmentation. In geomorphology, SfM photogrammetry enables the efficient creation of high-precision digital elevation models (DEMs) for laboratory flume studies, with innovations in automatic target detection, batch processing, and rigorous validation against laser scans [Articles - ESurf]. These advances demonstrate the versatility and precision of 3D reconstruction across disciplines.
gaps: Automated segmentation in dense, overlapping plant structures remains challenging
Scalability and robustness of workflows in highly variable field conditions are not fully addressed
Integration of deep learning for end-to-end automation is still underexplored
Cross-domain validation of 3D reconstruction accuracy (e.g., between plant science and geomorphology) is limited

web_summaries.sub_topic: Modern Control Theory in Agricultural Engineering
web_summaries.summary: Modern control theory is increasingly integrated into agricultural engineering curricula and research, reflecting the sector's shift toward automation, precision, and data-driven management. Graduate programs in Agricultural and Biological Engineering, such as those at the University of Florida, now offer courses that blend classical and modern control approaches, including fuzzy logic and neural networks, with application programming and simulation techniques. These courses emphasize both theoretical foundations and practical skills, preparing students to address complex, dynamic systems in agriculture through advanced modeling, automation, and robotics [Graduate Courses - Agricultural and Biological Engineering][Agricultural and Biological Engineering - Graduate Catalog]. Complementary offerings in Electrical and Computer Engineering at institutions like Texas A&M University further reinforce this trend, with coursework on state-space control theory and engineering applications relevant to communication networks and smart systems [ECEN - Texas A&M University Catalogs]. This interdisciplinary approach is essential for developing robust, adaptive control systems tailored to the unique challenges of agricultural environments.
gaps: Limited real-world case studies on deploying modern control systems in diverse agricultural settings
Integration of advanced control theory with emerging sensor and phenotyping technologies remains underexplored
Scalability and robustness of modern control algorithms in variable field conditions need further research

web_summaries.summary: Recent advances in mathematical modeling of crop grains leverage high-throughput imaging and computational analysis to extract detailed phenotypic traits, supporting breeding and genetic research. Structured light imaging and 3D point cloud analysis enable precise measurement of grain shape, size, and volume, with machine learning models (e.g., XGBoost, SVM) achieving high accuracy in classifying filled/unfilled grains and subspecies, though variety-level classification remains challenging [Cereal grain 3D point cloud analysis method for shape extraction ...]. Digital image analysis has proven effective for rapid assessment of kernel shape and color in wheat, revealing that gene bank accessions offer higher protein and gluten content but lower yield and carotenoid concentration compared to modern cultivars; color traits correlate more strongly with processing quality than shape [A comparison of phenotypic variation in Triticum durum Desf ...][A comparison of phenotypic variation in Triticum durum Desf ...]. Advanced phenotyping platforms integrate non-destructive imaging (visible, IR, hyperspectral, CT, MRI) and sophisticated statistical methods (PCA, clustering, growth modeling) to analyze large-scale, high-dimensional datasets, facilitating the linkage of phenotypes to genotypes for crop improvement [Advanced phenotyping and phenotype data analysis for the study of ...].
gaps: Limited accuracy in variety-level grain classification using current models
Need for improved integration of multi-modal imaging data for comprehensive trait analysis
Lack of cost-effective, scalable data analysis infrastructures for large-scale phenomics
Insufficient statistical methods for handling high-dimensional phenotypic datasets

web_summaries.summary: Recent advances in phenotypic trait extraction and analysis leverage 3D imaging and point cloud processing to improve accuracy and throughput in crop research. A comprehensive workflow for wheat phenotyping utilizes multi-view image sequences and 3D reconstruction (SFM-MVS) to generate dense point clouds, enabling automated extraction of traits such as plant height, leaf length, and width with high accuracy (R² > 0.995) compared to manual measurements. This method also supports dynamic monitoring of growth and statistical analysis of cultivar differences, though segmentation is challenged by dense leaf overlap, suggesting future improvements with neural networks [An integrated method for phenotypic analysis of wheat based on ...]. For cereal grains, structured light imaging combined with point cloud analysis enables high-throughput extraction of 25 shape traits and machine learning-based classification of filled/unfilled grains and rice subspecies, achieving high accuracy and efficiency (e.g., XGBoost: 90.18% for filled/unfilled, 99.95% for indica/japonica) [Cereal grain 3D point cloud analysis method for shape extraction ...]. These approaches represent significant advances for breeding, genetic research, and precision agriculture.
gaps: Robust segmentation methods for overlapping plant organs in dense canopies
Integration of neural network-based segmentation for improved accuracy
Generalization of 3D phenotyping workflows across diverse crop species
Automated trait extraction for complex or irregular plant structures

web_summaries.summary: Recent advances in sensor technologies have enabled non-destructive, high-throughput phenotyping and trait analysis in seeds and granular materials. Structured light imaging, as demonstrated in wheat grain analysis, allows for precise 3D point cloud acquisition, enabling extraction of detailed phenotypic traits and ventral sulcus features with mean absolute percentage errors below 5% for key measurements. This method, validated against X-ray CT and manual measurements, offers comparable accuracy with greater efficiency and is integrated into user-friendly software for automated trait extraction and weight prediction using machine learning models [An Intelligent Analysis Method for 3D Wheat Grain and Ventral ... (Frontiers), An Intelligent Analysis Method for 3D Wheat Grain and Ventral ... (PMC)]. Photogrammetry-based frameworks, employing Structure from Motion (SfM), have proven effective for volume and density estimation of porous aggregates, achieving sub-millimeter accuracy and closely matching CT-derived values, while outperforming traditional water displacement methods [Photogrammetry-Based Volume Measurement Framework for ... - PMC]. Additionally, 2D and 3D photogrammetry datasets of granular heaps facilitate standardized calibration and validation of measurement techniques [2D and 3D Scans of Granular Rock Material Heaps and Calibration ...]. For maize, hyperspectral imaging and machine vision, combined with machine learning, enable non-destructive vigor assessment and gene-trait association, achieving up to 90% classification accuracy [Non-destructive detection strategy of maize seed vigor based ... - PMC].
gaps: Integration of multi-modal sensor data (e.g., hyperspectral, 3D, thermal) for comprehensive trait analysis remains underexplored.
Scalability and automation of sensor-based phenotyping for large-scale breeding programs need further development.
Robustness of sensor technologies across diverse crop species and environmental conditions is not fully validated.
Standardized protocols for cross-platform calibration and data interoperability are lacking.

web_summaries.summary: System identification and validation are critical for developing robust measurement and diagnostic systems in agriculture and related fields. A high-throughput, non-destructive 3D reconstruction system for plant phenotyping was validated using a shape-from-silhouette approach with multiple cameras, achieving high correlation with manual ground truth for key plant features such as stem and leaf dimensions. The system demonstrated rapid processing (20–60 ms per reconstruction) and suitability for large-scale phenotyping, though it faced challenges with geometric approximations and segmentation errors, especially for complex leaf structures [Validation of plant part measurements using a 3D reconstruction ...]. In the context of disease diagnosis, a nonlinear fuzzy rank-based ensemble (NL-FuRBE) method combined with advanced image enhancement techniques significantly improved the accuracy and efficiency of citrus leaf disease identification, outperforming traditional and state-of-the-art methods and enabling real-time, scalable diagnosis [Precision diagnosis of citrus leaf diseases using image ...]. Together, these studies highlight the importance of integrating advanced computational methods and rigorous validation for reliable system identification in agricultural applications.
gaps: Limited validation on diverse plant species and complex morphologies
Need for improved segmentation algorithms for intricate structures
Scalability and robustness of identification methods in field conditions
Integration of multi-modal data for enhanced system identification

web_summaries.summary: Recent advances in 3D reconstruction and imaging have enabled significant progress in both plant and metal phenotyping applications. In plant science, a comprehensive workflow using Structure-from-Motion and Multi-View Stereo (SFM-MVS) was developed for wheat, allowing non-destructive, high-throughput extraction of key phenotypic traits across growth stages. This method achieved high accuracy in measuring plant height, leaf length, and width, and effectively differentiated cultivars, though challenges remain in segmenting overlapping leaves, suggesting neural network-based improvements [An integrated method for phenotypic analysis of wheat based on ...]. For oilseed rape, a deep learning pipeline using StarDist-3D on micro-CT images automated seed detection and trait extraction within pods, outperforming manual methods and offering generalizability to other species [Automated extraction of pod phenotype data from micro-computed ...]. In metal additive manufacturing, high-intensity ultrasound during 3D printing of Ti-6Al-4V transformed grain structure from columnar to fine equiaxed grains, improving mechanical properties and demonstrating scalability to other alloys [Grain structure control during metal 3D printing by high-intensity ...].
gaps: Automated segmentation of overlapping plant organs in dense canopies
Integration of neural network-based segmentation into 3D plant phenotyping pipelines
Scalability and generalization of deep learning pipelines to diverse crop species and structures
Industrial-scale implementation and process control of ultrasound-assisted grain refinement in metal AM
Long-term effects of grain structure modification on component reliability

final_summaries.sub_topic: System identification and validation in agricultural engineering for crop analysis
final_summaries.summary: Recent advancements in system identification for crop analysis leverage deep learning and explainable AI to enhance accuracy and interpretability. Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) have demonstrated high performance in classifying rice grain varieties and diagnosing leaf diseases, with explainability techniques like SHAP and LIME providing transparency into model decisions [ref2]. For crop pattern recognition, Deep Neural Networks (DNNs) outperform traditional classifiers such as Naive Bayes and Random Forest, especially when applied to time series remote sensing data [ref3]. In wheat crop analysis, hyperspectral imaging (HSI) combined with deep learning enables non-destructive, efficient health assessment, though challenges remain due to high data dimensionality and limited labeled samples [ref5]. Spatio-temporal crop classification benefits from hybrid CNN architectures that integrate 3D and 1D convolutions, achieving superior accuracy and efficiency on benchmark datasets [ref7]. Transformer-based models introduce self-attention mechanisms for crop-type classification, but their explainability is limited by dependencies on training data composition, underscoring the need for more robust temporal dynamic learning [ref16].
gaps: Limited generalizability and interpretability of deep learning models across diverse crop types and regions
Challenges in handling high-dimensional hyperspectral data with scarce labeled samples
Insufficient integration of temporal vegetation dynamics in current explainability frameworks
Lack of standardized benchmarks for cross-comparison of system identification methods in crop analysis

final_summaries.sub_topic: 3D reconstruction methods for crop grains in agricultural engineering
final_summaries.summary: Recent advancements in agricultural engineering have leveraged high-resolution UAV imagery and deep learning frameworks to enhance the detection and monitoring of crop changes at a fine-grained level. The AGSPNet framework introduces a novel approach by integrating agricultural geographic scene constraints and parcel-scale segmentation to address challenges such as spectral confusion, complex backgrounds, and noise in UAV images. This method comprises three core modules: agricultural geographic scene division, parcel edge extraction, and a crop semantic change detection (SCD) module. The introduction of the CSCD dataset, specifically tailored for agricultural monitoring, enables robust evaluation of semantic variation types in diverse geographical contexts. Experimental results demonstrate that AGSPNet significantly outperforms existing deep learning SCD models, achieving notable improvements in F1-score, kappa, overall accuracy, and mean intersection over union. These advancements provide a foundation for more accurate crop monitoring, yield prediction, and informed agricultural management, supporting the broader goals of smart agriculture and food security [ref1].
gaps: Integration of 3D reconstruction techniques with semantic change detection for crop grains remains unexplored.
Limited evaluation on diverse crop types and varying environmental conditions.
Real-time deployment and scalability of the proposed framework in large-scale agricultural settings.
Fusion of multi-modal data (e.g., LiDAR, multispectral) for enhanced 3D crop grain modeling.

final_summaries.sub_topic: Sensor technologies and data acquisition for 3D crop grain phenotyping
final_summaries.summary: Recent advances in sensor technologies and data acquisition architectures, though primarily developed for high-throughput physics experiments, offer valuable insights for 3D crop grain phenotyping. The HAWC gamma-ray observatory demonstrates a scalable, dead-time-free data acquisition system using photomultiplier tubes, TDCs, and high-throughput real-time processing, achieving minimal latency and robust parallel data analysis [ref14]. Similarly, the COMPASS DataBase (CDB) integrates heterogeneous data sources and acquisition hardware, leveraging open-source technologies for scalable, distributed, and platform-independent data storage and retrieval, with automated post-processing capabilities [ref5]. The Baikal-GVD project proposes a fiber optic-based data acquisition system, enabling increased throughput and flexible triggering, validated through dedicated test facilities [ref3]. The KM3NeT observatory’s modular Control Unit software ensures resilient, dynamic resource provisioning and failover, supporting continuous data acquisition even under network disruptions [ref1]. Collectively, these systems highlight the importance of modularity, scalability, and real-time processing in designing robust sensor and data acquisition platforms for complex, high-volume phenotyping applications.
gaps: Adaptation of high-throughput physics DAQ architectures to agricultural phenotyping sensor networks
Integration of multi-modal sensor data (e.g., imaging, spectroscopy) for 3D crop grain analysis
Real-time, in-field data processing and feedback for phenotyping applications
Scalable, open-source frameworks tailored to agricultural data acquisition and storage needs

final_summaries.summary: 3D reconstruction forms the backbone of modern phenotypic analysis and environmental modeling, leveraging techniques such as Structure-from-Motion (SfM) and Multi-View Stereo (MVS) to generate dense point clouds from multi-view image sequences. In plant science, integrated workflows have been developed for full-cycle phenotypic analysis of wheat, enabling non-destructive, high-throughput extraction of traits like plant height, leaf length, and width with high accuracy (R² > 0.995) [An integrated method for phenotypic analysis of wheat based on ..., An integrated method for phenotypic analysis of wheat based ... - PMC]. These methods involve careful image acquisition, advanced point cloud preprocessing (e.g., improved Euclidean clustering, color/statistical filtering), and organ segmentation (region growing algorithms). Challenges such as dense tillering and overlapping organs remain, but future directions include neural network-based segmentation. In geomorphology, SfM photogrammetry enables the efficient creation of high-precision digital elevation models (DEMs) for laboratory flume studies, with innovations in automatic target detection, batch processing, and rigorous validation against laser scans [Articles - ESurf]. These advances demonstrate the versatility and precision of 3D reconstruction across disciplines.
gaps: Automated segmentation in dense, overlapping plant structures remains challenging
Scalability and robustness of workflows in highly variable field conditions are not fully addressed
Integration of deep learning for end-to-end automation is still underexplored
Cross-domain validation of 3D reconstruction accuracy (e.g., between plant science and geomorphology) is limited

final_summaries.sub_topic: Modern Control Theory in Agricultural Engineering
final_summaries.summary: Modern control theory is increasingly integrated into agricultural engineering curricula and research, reflecting the sector's shift toward automation, precision, and data-driven management. Graduate programs in Agricultural and Biological Engineering, such as those at the University of Florida, now offer courses that blend classical and modern control approaches, including fuzzy logic and neural networks, with application programming and simulation techniques. These courses emphasize both theoretical foundations and practical skills, preparing students to address complex, dynamic systems in agriculture through advanced modeling, automation, and robotics [Graduate Courses - Agricultural and Biological Engineering][Agricultural and Biological Engineering - Graduate Catalog]. Complementary offerings in Electrical and Computer Engineering at institutions like Texas A&M University further reinforce this trend, with coursework on state-space control theory and engineering applications relevant to communication networks and smart systems [ECEN - Texas A&M University Catalogs]. This interdisciplinary approach is essential for developing robust, adaptive control systems tailored to the unique challenges of agricultural environments.
gaps: Limited real-world case studies on deploying modern control systems in diverse agricultural settings
Integration of advanced control theory with emerging sensor and phenotyping technologies remains underexplored
Scalability and robustness of modern control algorithms in variable field conditions need further research

final_summaries.summary: Recent advances in mathematical modeling of crop grains leverage high-throughput imaging and computational analysis to extract detailed phenotypic traits, supporting breeding and genetic research. Structured light imaging and 3D point cloud analysis enable precise measurement of grain shape, size, and volume, with machine learning models (e.g., XGBoost, SVM) achieving high accuracy in classifying filled/unfilled grains and subspecies, though variety-level classification remains challenging [Cereal grain 3D point cloud analysis method for shape extraction ...]. Digital image analysis has proven effective for rapid assessment of kernel shape and color in wheat, revealing that gene bank accessions offer higher protein and gluten content but lower yield and carotenoid concentration compared to modern cultivars; color traits correlate more strongly with processing quality than shape [A comparison of phenotypic variation in Triticum durum Desf ...][A comparison of phenotypic variation in Triticum durum Desf ...]. Advanced phenotyping platforms integrate non-destructive imaging (visible, IR, hyperspectral, CT, MRI) and sophisticated statistical methods (PCA, clustering, growth modeling) to analyze large-scale, high-dimensional datasets, facilitating the linkage of phenotypes to genotypes for crop improvement [Advanced phenotyping and phenotype data analysis for the study of ...].
gaps: Limited accuracy in variety-level grain classification using current models
Need for improved integration of multi-modal imaging data for comprehensive trait analysis
Lack of cost-effective, scalable data analysis infrastructures for large-scale phenomics
Insufficient statistical methods for handling high-dimensional phenotypic datasets

final_summaries.summary: Recent advances in phenotypic trait extraction and analysis leverage 3D imaging and point cloud processing to improve accuracy and throughput in crop research. A comprehensive workflow for wheat phenotyping utilizes multi-view image sequences and 3D reconstruction (SFM-MVS) to generate dense point clouds, enabling automated extraction of traits such as plant height, leaf length, and width with high accuracy (R² > 0.995) compared to manual measurements. This method also supports dynamic monitoring of growth and statistical analysis of cultivar differences, though segmentation is challenged by dense leaf overlap, suggesting future improvements with neural networks [An integrated method for phenotypic analysis of wheat based on ...]. For cereal grains, structured light imaging combined with point cloud analysis enables high-throughput extraction of 25 shape traits and machine learning-based classification of filled/unfilled grains and rice subspecies, achieving high accuracy and efficiency (e.g., XGBoost: 90.18% for filled/unfilled, 99.95% for indica/japonica) [Cereal grain 3D point cloud analysis method for shape extraction ...]. These approaches represent significant advances for breeding, genetic research, and precision agriculture.
gaps: Robust segmentation methods for overlapping plant organs in dense canopies
Integration of neural network-based segmentation for improved accuracy
Generalization of 3D phenotyping workflows across diverse crop species
Automated trait extraction for complex or irregular plant structures

final_summaries.summary: Recent advances in sensor technologies have enabled non-destructive, high-throughput phenotyping and trait analysis in seeds and granular materials. Structured light imaging, as demonstrated in wheat grain analysis, allows for precise 3D point cloud acquisition, enabling extraction of detailed phenotypic traits and ventral sulcus features with mean absolute percentage errors below 5% for key measurements. This method, validated against X-ray CT and manual measurements, offers comparable accuracy with greater efficiency and is integrated into user-friendly software for automated trait extraction and weight prediction using machine learning models [An Intelligent Analysis Method for 3D Wheat Grain and Ventral ... (Frontiers), An Intelligent Analysis Method for 3D Wheat Grain and Ventral ... (PMC)]. Photogrammetry-based frameworks, employing Structure from Motion (SfM), have proven effective for volume and density estimation of porous aggregates, achieving sub-millimeter accuracy and closely matching CT-derived values, while outperforming traditional water displacement methods [Photogrammetry-Based Volume Measurement Framework for ... - PMC]. Additionally, 2D and 3D photogrammetry datasets of granular heaps facilitate standardized calibration and validation of measurement techniques [2D and 3D Scans of Granular Rock Material Heaps and Calibration ...]. For maize, hyperspectral imaging and machine vision, combined with machine learning, enable non-destructive vigor assessment and gene-trait association, achieving up to 90% classification accuracy [Non-destructive detection strategy of maize seed vigor based ... - PMC].

Standardized protocols for cross-platform calibration and data interoperability are lacking.

final_summaries.summary: System identification and validation are critical for developing robust measurement and diagnostic systems in agriculture and related fields. A high-throughput, non-destructive 3D reconstruction system for plant phenotyping was validated using a shape-from-silhouette approach with multiple cameras, achieving high correlation with manual ground truth for key plant features such as stem and leaf dimensions. The system demonstrated rapid processing (20–60 ms per reconstruction) and suitability for large-scale phenotyping, though it faced challenges with geometric approximations and segmentation errors, especially for complex leaf structures [Validation of plant part measurements using a 3D reconstruction ...]. In the context of disease diagnosis, a nonlinear fuzzy rank-based ensemble (NL-FuRBE) method combined with advanced image enhancement techniques significantly improved the accuracy and efficiency of citrus leaf disease identification, outperforming traditional and state-of-the-art methods and enabling real-time, scalable diagnosis [Precision diagnosis of citrus leaf diseases using image ...]. Together, these studies highlight the importance of integrating advanced computational methods and rigorous validation for reliable system identification in agricultural applications.
gaps: Limited validation on diverse plant species and complex morphologies
Need for improved segmentation algorithms for intricate structures
Scalability and robustness of identification methods in field conditions
Integration of multi-modal data for enhanced system identification

final_composition.title: Design Report: Modern Control-Theoretic Modeling, Analysis, and Design for 3D Reconstruction & Phenotypic Analysis of Crop Grains
final_composition.executive_summary: We know that multi-view 3D reconstruction (SfM–MVS) and structured-light point-cloud pipelines can extract plant and grain phenotypes with high measured agreement to ground truth, and that ML (e.g., XGBoost) can classify grain states/subspecies at high accuracy under controlled data conditions [1][2][3]. What’s most constrained now is robust segmentation/identification under occlusion and variability; for example, wheat trait extraction from SfM–MVS point clouds achieved R² > 0.995 (2024, “An integrated method for phenotypic analysis of wheat…”) but still reports overlap-driven segmentation challenges [1]. Over the next ≤10 years, progress is most likely from tighter closed-loop data acquisition + standardized calibration datasets + more generalizable validation across sensors and environments, borrowing modular, resilient DAQ/control patterns from high-throughput systems [4][5].
final_composition.scope:
This report focuses on modeling, system identification/validation, and control-theoretic design choices for research workflows that reconstruct 3D geometry of crop grains (and related plant structures) and extract phenotypic traits.

Included (only where supported by provided items):
- 3D reconstruction workflows (SfM–MVS; structured light; shape-from-silhouette) and quantitative validation outcomes [1][2][3].
- Trait extraction and ML-based classification results for grains/seeds (accuracy metrics and measured errors where reported) [2][3][6].
- Remote-sensing/UAV semantic change detection as a complementary modeling layer for agricultural scenes (AGSPNet/CSCD) [7].
- Data acquisition and control-unit/DAQ architectural patterns (modularity, failover, throughput) as design analogs for phenotyping sensor networks (background due to age) [4][5][8][9].

Out of scope (not sufficiently quantified in items): new performance claims, unreported algorithmic comparisons, cost estimates, or timelines not explicitly named in sources.
final_composition.methodology:
Assembled from the provided, validated summary items with these rules:
- Only statements directly supported by items are included.
- Quantitative performance statements are tied to a named method/experiment and year (e.g., R², accuracy %, processing time, MAPE) [1][2][3][6].
- Sources older than ~18 months are marked as (background) in references and treated as design patterns rather than current state-of-the-art evidence [4][5][8][9].
- Where items highlight limitations (e.g., segmentation under overlap, limited labeled hyperspectral data, limited generalization), those are propagated into “Limiting Backgrounds & Systematics”, gaps, and recommendations [1][10][11].
final_composition.glossary:
- **SfM (Structure-from-Motion):** Multi-view geometry method estimating camera poses and sparse 3D structure from overlapping images; often paired with MVS for dense reconstruction [1].
- **MVS (Multi-View Stereo):** Densifies 3D reconstructions by estimating depth/points from multiple registered views [1].
- **Point cloud:** Set of 3D points representing a surface/volume (often with color/intensity), used for trait extraction [1][2].
- **Structured light imaging:** Active 3D scanning projecting patterns to recover dense depth/point clouds; used for grain trait measurement [3].
- **Shape-from-silhouette:** 3D reconstruction using multi-view silhouettes to approximate volume; validated for plant part measurement [6].
- **System identification/validation:** Building and verifying models (measurement or predictive) against ground truth data; includes calibration, uncertainty checks, and cross-domain tests [6][10].
- **Explainable AI (XAI):** Methods (e.g., SHAP/LIME as cited in the items) to interpret model decisions; noted as important but limited in temporal/dynamic integration in crop tasks [10].
- **Semantic change detection (SCD):** Detecting and labeling changes between images over time; AGSPNet targets parcel-scale crop SCD from UAV imagery [7].
- **DAQ (data acquisition):** Hardware/software pipeline for sensing, time-stamping, transport, storage, and online processing; used here as an architectural analogy for phenotyping systems (background) [4][5][8][9].
final_composition.sota:
## 3.1 At-a-Glance Artifact

| Category (most represented in items) | Representative mass range | Strongest current constraint (number, unit, mass, experiment, year) | Main detection channels | Near-term prospects (≤10 yrs) with named experiments |
|---|---:|---|---|---|
